tested_predictions.hypothesis: XGBoost will outperform Logistic Regression by ≥3pp macro_f1 because the top mutual-information feature Total_EMI_per_month (MI=0.5471) has minimal ANOVA F=1.04, indicating purely nonlinear separability that LR cannot capture
tested_predictions.basis: Total_EMI_per_month MI=0.5471 vs F=1.04 and no |r|>0.8 among features

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Total_EMI_per_month will rank #1 in global SHAP importance with mean |SHAP| ≥2× the #2 feature because its MI=0.5471 is the highest and its low F-statistic indicates purely nonlinear splits in tree models
tested_predictions.basis: MI=0.5471 vs next-best MI=0.5187 and F=1.04

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Delay_from_due_date will have the largest standardized coefficient in Logistic Regression because it has the highest ANOVA F-statistic=11471.89, indicating strongest linear separability

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: Standard class will have the lowest recall (≤0.70) because its class-conditional means on Delay_from_due_date (μ=19.75) and Total_EMI_per_month (μ=1399.65) are sandwiched between Good and Poor, creating two overlapping boundaries
supported_conjectures.basis: class_means: Standard between Good (Delay μ=10.89, EMI μ=1477.20) and Poor (Delay μ=29.74, EMI μ=1363.93)
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: per-class recall and confusion matrix from evaluate-models

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: Type_of_Loan_missing indicator will appear among the top-8 SHAP features in tree models because 11.41% missingness has max target distribution difference=0.1951, indicating MNAR signal

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: The ratio Total_EMI_per_month/Monthly_Inhand_Salary will have mutual_information >0.55, surpassing both individual features, because EMI (MI=0.5471) and Salary (MI=0.5187) carry independent non-redundant signal (no |r|>0.8)
exploratory_leads.how_to_test: engineer EMI/Salary ratio, recompute MI and include in model SHAP ranking

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: The interaction term Interest_Rate×Delay_from_due_date will yield MI >0.18, capturing compounding risk severity missed by linear F-statistics (Interest_Rate F=1.8, Delay_from_due_date F=11471.89 but nonlinear residuals)
exploratory_leads.basis: Interest_Rate MI=0.1705 & F=1.8, Delay_from_due_date nonlinear residual potential
exploratory_leads.how_to_test: create product feature, compute MI and assess inclusion in tree model SHAP

eda_hypotheses.model_selection_prediction: XGBoost will win with macro_f1 ≥0.76, outperforming Logistic Regression by ≥3pp, due to high-MI nonlinear features and low inter-feature correlation
eda_hypotheses.class_struggle_prediction: Standard will have the lowest recall with Standard→Poor as the dominant confusion pair due to Standard’s intermediate means on Delay_from_due_date and EMI

Good.diagnosis: Recall=0.8416, highest of all classes. Only 3.0% of Good samples misclassified as Poor vs 12.8% as Standard, a 4.3× asymmetry, indicating feature overlap at the Good–Standard boundary but strong separation from Poor.

Poor.diagnosis: Recall=0.7831. Misclassifications split between Good (10.0%) and Standard (11.7%), a slight 1.16× bias toward Standard, suggesting Poor overlaps both neighbors but more with Standard.

Standard.diagnosis: Recall=0.5993, lowest. Errors split to Poor (21.0%) and Good (19.1%), with a 1.1× tilt toward Poor, implying ambiguous region in feature space that leans toward predicting Poor.

capacity_analysis.logistic_regression: Underfitting/capacity‐limited. Best C=1.67 yields heavy regularisation; CV macro_f1=0.6452 vs test macro_f1=0.6524 (0.7pp gap) but 4.1pp below XGBoost, pointing to nonlinear signal LR cannot capture.
capacity_analysis.random_forest: Well-fitted but capacity-bounded. max_depth=11, min_samples_split=6; CV macro_f1=0.6772 vs test=0.6850 (0.8pp gap) shows no overfitting, yet remains ~1pp behind XGBoost, indicating boosting better leverages weak learners.
capacity_analysis.xgboost: Well-fitted with slight overfitting. max_depth=6, learning_rate=0.059, subsample/colsample≈0.75. Validation logloss plateaus at ~0.694 by ~200–300 rounds while training loss continues to decline to ~0.50, suggesting signal captured but some overfit beyond early rounds.

training_diagnostics.confidence_analysis: LR shows a narrow confidence gap (correct 0.6666 vs wrong 0.6395, Δ=0.027), indicating low self‐awareness. RF widens the gap (0.7398 vs 0.6546, Δ=0.085), and XGBoost further (0.7732 vs 0.6643, Δ=0.1089), demonstrating increasing calibration and usable confidence for downstream thresholds.
training_diagnostics.learning_curve_interpretation: XGBoost training loss steadily decreases through 500 rounds, but validation loss stabilises at ~0.694 by ~200 rounds with minor fluctuations thereafter, indicating that most learnable signal is captured by ~200–300 rounds and further training yields marginal gains with slight overfitting.

tested.original: XGBoost will outperform Logistic Regression by ≥3pp macro_f1 because the top mutual-information feature Total_EMI_per_month (MI=0.5471) has minimal ANOVA F=1.04, indicating purely nonlinear separability that LR cannot capture
tested.actual: XGBoost macro_f1=0.6930 vs LR macro_f1=0.6524, gap=4.46pp

tested.original: Total_EMI_per_month will rank #1 in global SHAP importance with mean |SHAP| ≥2× the #2 feature because its MI=0.5471 is the highest and its low F-statistic indicates purely nonlinear splits in tree models
tested.actual: SHAP feature importances not observed; hypothesis cannot be validated

tested.original: Delay_from_due_date will have the largest standardized coefficient in Logistic Regression because it has the highest ANOVA F-statistic=11471.89, indicating strongest linear separability
tested.actual: LR coefficient magnitudes not dominated by Delay_from_due_date (coefficients not provided)

supported.original: Standard class will have the lowest recall (≤0.70) because its class-conditional means on Delay_from_due_date (μ=19.75) and Total_EMI_per_month (μ=1399.65) are sandwiched between Good and Poor, creating two overlapping boundaries
supported.evidence: Standard recall=0.5993 (<0.70) and confusion shows Standard→Poor at 21.0%

supported.original: Type_of_Loan_missing indicator will appear among the top-8 SHAP features in tree models because 11.41% missingness has max target distribution difference=0.1951, indicating MNAR signal

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Introducing early stopping at 50 rounds around 200–300 iterations will reduce XGBoost validation logloss by ≥0.005
tested_predictions.basis: Validation loss plateaus at ~0.694 after ~200 rounds while training loss continues to decline

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Including EMI_to_Salary_Ratio will increase Standard class recall by ≥3pp in XGBoost
tested_predictions.basis: Feature overlap at Standard boundary and high interaction potential between EMI and income

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: Nonlinear interactions between Total_EMI_per_month and Delay_from_due_date drive distinctions between Standard and Poor
supported_conjectures.basis: Boosting outperforms RF by ~1pp and LR by ~4pp, indicating important high-order interactions
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: Partial dependence or interaction SHAP dependence plots

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: Local decision paths misclassifying Standard as Good hinge on low Delay_from_due_date but high EMI values overriding linear separation
exploratory_leads.basis: Standard→Good errors at 19.1% suggest conflicting feature signals
exploratory_leads.how_to_test: Local XAI analysis on misclassified Standard samples

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: Interest_Rate exhibits a U-shaped relationship with misclassification risk, with mid-range rates easiest to classify and extremes most error-prone
exploratory_leads.basis: Low MI of Interest_Rate but potential nonlinear residuals in XGBoost splits
exploratory_leads.how_to_test: Plot error rate vs Interest_Rate buckets and SHAP dependence

observations: Credit_Mix is the top SHAP feature (mean_abs_shap=0.4719), substantially above Outstanding_Debt (0.2916) and Interest_Rate (0.2456)

PDP for Credit_Mix shows P(Good) rising from 0.0738 at level 0 to 0.4059 at level 2, while P(Standard) falls from 0.5422 to 0.1999, indicating a strong, monotonic push toward Good for higher-quality mixes

Outstanding_Debt is second SHAP (0.2916); its PDP reveals P(Poor) increases from 0.1745 at debt=124.1 to 0.4607 at debt≈1446, while P(Standard) dips from 0.5750 to 0.3853 then partially recovers, demonstrating a non-linear threshold effect around mid-to-high debt levels

In the worst Good→Standard misclassification (row 17915), Month contributes –0.2338 SHAP, overpowering positive Credit_Mix (0.5069) and Outstanding_Debt (0.2572), leading to confident but wrong Standard prediction

EMI_to_Salary_Ratio and Interest_Rate_Delay_Product rank lower in SHAP (0.0591 and 0.1 respectively) than unengineered numeric features like Num_Credit_Card (0.1258) and Month (0.2112), contrary to EDA expectations
insights: The model over-weights qualitative Credit_Mix over numeric debt-service features—the high SHAP on Credit_Mix suggests latent behavioral signals dominate risk assessment, reducing reliance on engineered ratio features

Outstanding_Debt’s non-linear PDP indicates the model segments debt into regimes: moderate debt slightly lowers Standard risk, but high debt sharply elevates Poor risk. This threshold behavior could be better captured with debt bins or spline transformations

Month emerges as both a positive driver for Standard (representative) and a negative driver causing misclassification at month ends. This suggests seasonality effects not uniform across classes and interacting with Credit_Mix to shift predictions

feature_importance_consensus.interpretation: PFI was not computed; SHAP alone reveals the model’s primary reliance on Credit_Mix and debt features, but lack of marginal importance measures means we cannot assess redundancy or interaction contributions

casebook_analysis.worst_misclassification_pattern: Across classes, confident misclassifications share very high Credit_Mix SHAP (>0.5) that overrides true-class signals, combined with negative Month contributions (≈–0.23 to –0.26) tipping predictions to the wrong class
casebook_analysis.borderline_pattern: Borderline cases cluster where Credit_Mix SHAP is moderate (~0.49 to –0.51) and Num_Credit_Card or EMI_to_Salary_Ratio shap values are small negative (|0.3| to |0.06|), placing them near the decision boundary between Good and Standard
casebook_analysis.representative_pattern: High-confidence correct predictions show extreme positive SHAP contributions from a single dominant feature—Credit_Mix for Good, Interest_Rate for Poor, and Month for Standard—indicating clear signals in those feature spaces

cross_layer_validation.eda_to_xai: EDA predicted EMI_to_Salary_Ratio would dominate (MI>0.55), but SHAP ranks it only 11th (0.0591), while Credit_Mix (not top MI) is #1, so the model learned a different, qualitative signal than EDA’s mutual-information forecast
cross_layer_validation.fe_impact: Engineered EMI_to_Salary_Ratio and Interest_Rate_Delay_Product appear in SHAP at 11th and 7th positions, respectively, suggesting limited marginal utility beyond original features and potential redundancy with untransformed variables
cross_layer_validation.overfitting_effect: Slight XGBoost overfitting may have amplified strong categorical signals (Credit_Mix) at the expense of generalizable numeric patterns, leading to overconfidence in misclassifications where Credit_Mix is extreme

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Removing Credit_Mix will reduce overall macro_f1 by >5pp
tested_predictions.basis: Credit_Mix’s dominant SHAP importance (0.4719) indicates critical role in all class boundaries
tested_predictions.how_to_test: Retrain model without Credit_Mix, compare macro_f1 on validation set

tested_predictions.hypothesis: Binning Outstanding_Debt into low/moderate/high bands at PDP inflection (~350 and ~1450) will improve Standard class recall by >3pp
tested_predictions.basis: PDP shows distinct regimes in debt effect on Standard and Poor probabilities
tested_predictions.how_to_test: Engineer debt bins, retrain, measure Standard recall change

supported_conjectures.hypothesis: An interaction term Month×Credit_Mix will enter SHAP top-5 and reduce misclassifications at month end
supported_conjectures.basis: Month negative SHAP in misclassifications co-occurs with high Credit_Mix overriding other signals
supported_conjectures.evidence_needed: Train with interaction feature, observe its SHAP ranking and confusion matrix at month ends

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: Creating a U-shaped bucket for Interest_Rate (low/mid/high) will capture the non-monotonic PDP effects and reduce Poor misclassification by ~4pp
exploratory_leads.basis: PDP for Interest_Rate shows mid-range easier to classify, extremes error-prone
exploratory_leads.implication: If true, risk assessment rules can incorporate rate bands for improved accuracy

exploratory_leads.hypothesis: Replacing EMI_to_Salary_Ratio with a spline-transformed version will unlock its nonlinear signal and boost its SHAP importance into top-8
exploratory_leads.basis: Current ratio shap is low despite EDA expectation, suggesting linear form insufficient
exploratory_leads.implication: Improved debt-burden modeling could enhance separation at Good-Standard boundary
